title: Reading and Use of English – Part 8
description: You are going to read an article about four people who volunteered at an animal shelter. For questions 47–56, choose from the people A–D. The people may be chosen more than once.

texts.A:
### Alice, The Morning Dog Walker
Alice signed up for the earliest walking shift because it fitted around her bakery job. [47]She soon discovered that the hardest part was not controlling strong dogs, but remembering which animals had to avoid each other on the narrow path.[/47] She made small colour-coded cards for the leads, a habit borrowed from organising trays in the bakery before dawn. [48]The shelter manager later asked her to train new walkers because her notes reduced mistakes during busy mornings.[/48] Alice expected the dogs to be grateful and playful, but some were too anxious to leave their kennels. The first time one nervous collie chose to follow her through the gate felt like a genuine victory. She now says walking is less about exercise than trust. Rainy days are still unpleasant, yet she likes the peaceful hour before visitors arrive and the kennels become noisy again with questions, barking, metal bowls and footsteps.
texts.B:
### Ben, The Cat Room Reader
Ben began volunteering after a librarian friend suggested that his calm voice might help shy cats get used to people. [49]Instead of trying to touch them, he sat on the floor and read aloud from old travel books until they approached on their own.[/49] He had imagined the work would be sentimental, but it required patience when cats hid for weeks behind laundry baskets and ignored every encouraging whisper. [50]He was surprised by how carefully every change in appetite or grooming had to be recorded, even when nothing dramatic seemed to happen.[/50] His favourite success was a black cat who first came out only during descriptions of storms at sea and lonely harbours. [55]Ben says the shelter altered his idea of companionship, because being useful sometimes meant doing almost nothing visibly.[/55] He still keeps his voice low at home, a habit his family finds amusing during phone calls and evening meals.
texts.C:
### Carmen, The Adoption Photographer
Carmen offered to photograph the animals after noticing that many online profiles used blurred phone pictures. [51]Her experience as a portrait photographer helped her wait for the exact second when a nervous animal looked curious rather than frightened.[/51] The sessions were not as simple as she expected, since dogs often ran toward the camera, kittens climbed into the light box, and volunteers laughed at the chaos. She became frustrated when people praised only the cutest animals, so she began writing captions that described quieter qualities. [52]A turning point came when an elderly dog was adopted because his picture showed him resting his head gently on a volunteer's knee.[/52] Carmen learned that a photograph could correct a first impression, not just decorate a website or make volunteers feel proud. She now keeps spare towels in her car because every good picture seems to involve mud, treats, and at least one scratched sleeve.
texts.D:
### Dev, The Laundry Helper
Dev chose laundry duty because he thought it would be straightforward and safely away from emotional decisions and difficult conversations. [53]He had not realised that clean blankets were needed constantly to prevent infections spreading between kennels.[/53] The endless washing machines and bags of damp towels bored him at first, until a nurse explained how scent and warmth helped frightened animals settle after treatment. [56]Dev's background in warehouse work helped him reorganise the shelves so staff could find bedding by size without searching through piles.[/56] [54]His most awkward lesson was accepting that some toys had to be thrown away, however loved they looked, because they could no longer be cleaned properly.[/54] He rarely meets adopters, but he likes imagining an animal sleeping on a fresh towel before leaving for a new home after weeks of uncertainty. Dev says invisible chores can be the kindest ones, even when nobody thanks the person folding blankets.

questions.question: mentions having to remember which animals could not be near each other?
questions.explanation: Alice had to remember which dogs needed to avoid each other on the narrow path.

questions.question: was later asked to teach others because their system made the work safer?
questions.explanation: Alice trained new walkers after her notes reduced mistakes.

questions.question: used reading aloud as a way to let animals approach voluntarily?
questions.explanation: Ben read aloud until shy cats came towards him on their own.

questions.question: was surprised by the detailed monitoring required for small changes in animal behaviour?
questions.explanation: Ben recorded changes in appetite or grooming even when they seemed minor.

questions.question: used professional timing to capture a more appealing expression?
questions.explanation: Carmen waited for the moment when an animal looked curious rather than frightened.

questions.question: mentions an adoption that happened because an image revealed a gentle quality?
questions.explanation: An elderly dog was adopted after a photo showed his gentleness.

questions.question: learned that a routine cleaning task had an important health purpose?
questions.explanation: Dev realised clean blankets helped prevent infections spreading.

questions.question: had to accept disposing of familiar objects for hygiene reasons?
questions.explanation: Dev learned some toys had to be thrown away because they could not be cleaned.

questions.question: says their understanding of companionship changed through quiet usefulness?
questions.explanation: Ben says being useful could mean doing almost nothing visibly.

questions.question: reorganised supplies using experience from a previous job?
questions.explanation: Dev used his warehouse background to organise bedding shelves by size.
